title: Content Provenance and Watermarking
concept: Techniques like invisible watermarking and content credentials help track whether an image was AI-generated, supporting transparency and trust.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of content provenance?

options.text: To track the origin and history of a piece of digital content
To make AI images look more colorful and bright
To speed up the download time of large files

exercises.explain: Content provenance provides a way to verify the origin of digital media, showing if it was captured by a camera or generated by AI.

cards.title: Real or AI?
cards.body: Have you ever seen an image online and wondered: 'Is this real, or did an AI make it?' As AI images become hyper-realistic, telling them apart is getting tougher. That's where digital labels come in!

cards.title: What is Content Provenance?
cards.body: Content provenance is like a receipt for digital media. It uses techniques like invisible watermarking (hiding secret patterns in pixels) and digital metadata to prove whether an image was created by a human or generated by an AI.

cards.title: The Digital Passport
cards.body: Think of a content watermark like a secret digital passport. When a traveler (an image) goes abroad, the passport gets stamped. Even if they change their clothes (crop the image or change colors), the passport stamp still proves where they came from.

cards.title: Real-World Labeling
cards.body: Many modern AI image generators now automatically inject invisible signals (like Google's SynthID) into the images they create. When you upload these images to social media, platforms can scan for this signal and automatically label them as 'Made with AI'.

cards.title: Myth: Crop it to Remove it
cards.body: People think digital watermarks can just be cropped out like a logo in the corner. In reality, modern invisible watermarking embeds tiny patterns throughout the entire image file, making it very hard to remove even if you edit or crop the photo!

cards.title: Traceable vs. Untraceable
cards.body: See how watermarked files carry hidden tags, unlike standard files that leave users guessing.

left.text: No extra info. Looks nice, but its origins are completely untraceable.

right.text: Contains invisible code proving it was generated by a specific AI model.

exercises.prompt: How do invisible watermarks look to the average human eye?

options.text: They place a giant, bright red stamp in the middle of the photo.
They are hidden in the image pixels and cannot be seen by the naked eye.
They make the image turn completely black and white.
They only work on printed paper photos, not digital files.

exercises.explain: Invisible watermarks are designed to be imperceptible to humans, ensuring the visual quality of the image remains clean and unaltered while still being detectable by scanners.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The main goal of content provenance is to increase trust and transparency online.
exercises.explain: Content provenance standards are designed to help users identify AI content, building online safety and transparency.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: To track where an image came from, tools embed invisible [blank] into the pixels.
exercises.explain: Invisible watermarks are hidden directly inside the data of an image, making them hard to see with the human eye but easy for computers to detect.

exercises.prompt: Which of these images is most likely AI-generated?

options.text: Image B (Missing metadata with distorted, melting fingers)

exercises.explain: The first image contains digital credentials verifying it was taken by a real camera. The second image lacks credentials and has weird distortions, indicating it was AI-generated.

exercises.prompt: If someone edits or crops an AI-generated image that has an invisible watermark, what is the main purpose of that watermark?

options.text: It stays embedded inside the image file to show it was AI-made.
It automatically deletes the image from the internet.
It physically changes the pixels so the image looks blurry.
It prevents anyone from downloading or copying the image.

exercises.explain: Invisible watermarking and digital credentials act like an unforgeable label. Even if an AI image is slightly cropped, compressed, or shared on social media, the embedded watermark metadata remains intact to prove its AI origins.
